reasoning: The 119th Congress, under Republican control in both chambers, has demonstrated a clearly deregulatory approach to artificial intelligence. In May 2025, the House advanced a budget reconciliation package with a 10-year moratorium explicitly prohibiting states from enforcing any substantive design, performance, data-handling, or civil liability requirements on AI models. Senator Ted Cruz is advancing an identical moratorium in the Senate. Currently, there are no viable federal proposals establishing substantive requirements, standards, or prohibitions on domestic AI development or systems. Bills that have passed or are advancing either address non-consensual deepfakes (TAKE IT DOWN Act) or AI exports to China (S.321), both of which are explicitly carved out by the resolution criteria as insufficient for 'substantive regulation of AI systems or their development.' Given the short time horizon to January 2026 and the strong momentum explicitly directed at preventing binding regulation, the likelihood of a substantive AI regulatory bill passing both chambers and becoming law is effectively nil. With a 3% crowd forecast, I predict 2%, which is the lowest valid probability boundary.
